concepts.name: Set
concepts.intuition_gloss: A bag of distinct things, where all that matters is what's in the bag.
concepts.formal_gloss: A collection of distinct objects, itself treated as a single object, with membership as the primitive relation.

concepts.name: Function
concepts.intuition_gloss: A machine that takes an input and reliably returns exactly one output.
concepts.formal_gloss: A relation f from set A to set B assigning to each element of A exactly one element of B.

concepts.name: Equivalence Relation
concepts.intuition_gloss: A principled way of saying two different things count as 'the same', which sorts everything into non-overlapping buckets.
concepts.formal_gloss: A binary relation that is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive, partitioning its domain into equivalence classes.

concepts.name: Axiom & Axiomatic System
concepts.intuition_gloss: The agreed starting rules of the game, from which everything else must be derived.
concepts.formal_gloss: A statement accepted without proof within a formal system, from which theorems are derived by rules of inference.

concepts.name: Cardinality
concepts.intuition_gloss: The size of a set measured by pairing-up, revealing that some infinities are bigger than others.
concepts.formal_gloss: The equivalence class of a set under bijection; two sets have equal cardinality iff a bijection exists between them.

concepts.name: Natural Numbers (N)
concepts.intuition_gloss: The counting numbers 0, 1, 2, 3, ... — the raw material of arithmetic.
concepts.formal_gloss: The smallest set containing 0 and closed under successor, as characterized by the Peano axioms.

concepts.name: Integers (Z)
concepts.intuition_gloss: The whole numbers extended with negatives, so every subtraction has an answer.
concepts.formal_gloss: The ring obtained from N by adjoining additive inverses; formally, equivalence classes of pairs of naturals under (a,b) ~ (c,d) iff a+d = b+c.

concepts.name: Rational Numbers (Q)
concepts.intuition_gloss: All fractions — the numbers you can name with a ratio of two integers.
concepts.formal_gloss: The field of fractions of Z: equivalence classes of integer pairs (p,q), q nonzero, under (p,q) ~ (r,s) iff ps = qr.

concepts.name: Prime Number
concepts.intuition_gloss: The atoms of the integers — numbers that refuse to be broken into smaller factors.
concepts.formal_gloss: An integer greater than 1 whose only positive divisors are 1 and itself.

concepts.name: Modular Arithmetic
concepts.intuition_gloss: Clock arithmetic — numbers wrap around after reaching a fixed modulus.
concepts.formal_gloss: Arithmetic on the equivalence classes of integers under congruence mod n, forming the ring Z/nZ.

concepts.name: Complex Numbers (C)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Numbers living in a plane rather than on a line, where multiplication rotates and scales.
concepts.formal_gloss: The algebraically closed field R[i] with i^2 = -1; pairs of reals with (a,b)(c,d) = (ac-bd, ad+bc).

concepts.name: Polynomial
concepts.intuition_gloss: An expression built from variables using only addition and multiplication — the simplest interesting functions.
concepts.formal_gloss: A finite formal sum of terms c_k x^k with coefficients in a ring, forming the polynomial ring R[x].

concepts.name: Group
concepts.intuition_gloss: The algebra of reversible actions — any collection of moves you can compose and undo.
concepts.formal_gloss: A set with an associative binary operation, an identity element, and an inverse for every element.

concepts.name: Ring
concepts.intuition_gloss: A number-system-like world where you can add, subtract, and multiply, but maybe not divide.
concepts.formal_gloss: An abelian group under addition equipped with an associative multiplication that distributes over addition.

concepts.name: Field
concepts.intuition_gloss: A number system with all four operations working: add, subtract, multiply, and divide by anything nonzero.
concepts.formal_gloss: A commutative ring in which every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse.

concepts.name: Isomorphism
concepts.intuition_gloss: A perfect relabeling showing two structures are the same thing wearing different costumes.
concepts.formal_gloss: A structure-preserving bijection between two objects whose inverse also preserves structure.

concepts.name: Vector
concepts.intuition_gloss: An arrow with length and direction — or equally, a list of numbers acting as one object.
concepts.formal_gloss: An element of a vector space; concretely in R^n, an ordered n-tuple of real numbers.

concepts.name: Matrix
concepts.intuition_gloss: A grid of numbers that acts on vectors, encoding a linear transformation as data.
concepts.formal_gloss: A rectangular array over a field with entrywise addition and row-by-column multiplication, representing a linear map in chosen bases.

concepts.name: Vector Space
concepts.intuition_gloss: Any world where things can be added together and scaled, like arrows, polynomials, or signals.
concepts.formal_gloss: An abelian group with a scalar multiplication by a field satisfying compatibility and distributivity axioms.

concepts.name: Linear Transformation
concepts.intuition_gloss: A map that keeps grid lines parallel and evenly spaced — no bending, only stretching, rotating, shearing.
concepts.formal_gloss: A map T between vector spaces with T(u+v) = T(u)+T(v) and T(cv) = cT(v).

concepts.name: Eigenvalue & Eigenvector
concepts.intuition_gloss: The special directions a transformation merely stretches, and the stretch factors — its hidden skeleton.
concepts.formal_gloss: A nonzero vector v and scalar λ with Tv = λv for a linear operator T.

concepts.name: Coordinate System
concepts.intuition_gloss: An addressing scheme that turns points in space into lists of numbers, marrying geometry to algebra.
concepts.formal_gloss: A bijection between points of a space and tuples of numbers, e.g. Cartesian coordinates identifying the plane with R^2.

concepts.name: Symmetry & Transformation
concepts.intuition_gloss: A move you can make that leaves an object looking exactly as it was.
concepts.formal_gloss: An invertible structure-preserving map from an object to itself; the symmetries of an object form a group.

concepts.name: Manifold
concepts.intuition_gloss: A possibly-curved space that looks flat if you zoom in far enough, like the Earth's surface.
concepts.formal_gloss: A topological space locally homeomorphic to R^n, usually with smooth transition maps between overlapping charts.

concepts.name: Metric Space
concepts.intuition_gloss: Any world equipped with a sensible notion of distance between its points.
concepts.formal_gloss: A set with a function d satisfying nonnegativity, identity of indiscernibles, symmetry, and the triangle inequality.

concepts.name: Topological Space
concepts.intuition_gloss: A space where 'nearness' is defined without any ruler, by declaring which sets count as open neighborhoods.
concepts.formal_gloss: A set with a collection of open subsets closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections, containing the empty set and the whole space.

concepts.name: Real Numbers (R)
concepts.intuition_gloss: The complete number line with no gaps — every point you can approach is actually there.
concepts.formal_gloss: The unique complete ordered field; constructed as Dedekind cuts or equivalence classes of Cauchy sequences of rationals.

concepts.name: Sequence
concepts.intuition_gloss: An infinite ordered list of numbers, often marching toward a destination.
concepts.formal_gloss: A function from N to a set, written (a_n); the basic carrier of limiting processes.

concepts.name: Limit
concepts.intuition_gloss: The value a process homes in on — as close as you like, if you go far enough.
concepts.formal_gloss: L is the limit of a_n if for every ε > 0 there exists N such that |a_n - L| < ε for all n > N.

concepts.name: Continuity
concepts.intuition_gloss: A function you can draw without lifting your pen — small input nudges cause only small output nudges.
concepts.formal_gloss: f is continuous at a if for every ε > 0 there is δ > 0 with |f(x) - f(a)| < ε whenever |x - a| < δ; topologically, preimages of open sets are open.

concepts.name: Derivative
concepts.intuition_gloss: The instantaneous rate of change — the slope of the best straight-line stand-in at a point.
concepts.formal_gloss: f'(a) = lim as h→0 of (f(a+h) - f(a))/h, when the limit exists; the linear map best approximating f near a.

concepts.name: Integral
concepts.intuition_gloss: Total accumulation — the area under a curve, built by summing infinitely many infinitesimal slivers.
concepts.formal_gloss: The limit of Riemann sums of f over ever-finer partitions of [a,b]; the antiderivative evaluated at the endpoints, by the fundamental theorem of calculus.

concepts.name: Differential Equation
concepts.intuition_gloss: A rule stating how something changes from moment to moment; solving it reveals the whole trajectory.
concepts.formal_gloss: An equation relating an unknown function to its derivatives; a solution is a function satisfying it on a domain.

concepts.name: Probability Space
concepts.intuition_gloss: The complete universe of possible outcomes of an experiment, with a weight assigned to every event.
concepts.formal_gloss: A triple (Ω, F, P) of a sample space, a σ-algebra of events, and a measure P with P(Ω) = 1.

concepts.name: Random Variable
concepts.intuition_gloss: A number whose value is decided by chance, like the sum showing on two dice.
concepts.formal_gloss: A measurable function from a probability space to the reals, inducing a distribution on R.

concepts.name: Expectation
concepts.intuition_gloss: The long-run average value of a random quantity over many repetitions.
concepts.formal_gloss: E[X] = integral of X with respect to the probability measure; for discrete X, the probability-weighted sum of its values.

concepts.name: Statistical Inference
concepts.intuition_gloss: Reasoning backwards from a sample you saw to the process that produced it, with honest error bars.
concepts.formal_gloss: The framework of estimating parameters and testing hypotheses about a distribution from observed data, with quantified uncertainty.

concepts.name: Counting Principles
concepts.intuition_gloss: Systematic ways to count arrangements without listing them — multiply choices, then correct for overcounting.
concepts.formal_gloss: The product and sum rules with permutations n!/(n-k)! and combinations n-choose-k as canonical consequences.

concepts.name: Graph
concepts.intuition_gloss: Dots joined by lines — the universal picture of any network of relationships.
concepts.formal_gloss: A pair (V, E) of a vertex set and a set of edges, each edge an unordered (or ordered) pair of vertices.

concepts.name: Algorithm
concepts.intuition_gloss: A recipe precise enough that a machine can follow it — finitely many steps, guaranteed to finish.
concepts.formal_gloss: A finite, effective procedure for computing a function, formalizable as a Turing machine.

concepts.name: Optimization
concepts.intuition_gloss: Finding the best option — the highest peak or lowest valley — subject to the constraints you must respect.
concepts.formal_gloss: The problem of finding x in a feasible set minimizing (or maximizing) an objective function f(x).

concepts.name: Mathematical Model
concepts.intuition_gloss: A deliberately simplified mathematical portrait of a real system, built to predict or explain.
concepts.formal_gloss: A formal structure (equations, graphs, probability distributions) whose variables and relations are mapped to features of a real-world system.

concepts.name: Proof
concepts.intuition_gloss: An airtight chain of reasoning that forces agreement — each link checkable by anyone.
concepts.formal_gloss: A finite sequence of statements, each an axiom, hypothesis, or consequence of earlier statements by valid inference, ending at the theorem.

concepts.name: Mathematical Induction
concepts.intuition_gloss: The domino argument: knock over the first, show each knocks over the next, and infinitely many fall.
concepts.formal_gloss: If P(0) holds and P(n) implies P(n+1) for all n, then P(n) holds for every natural number n.

concepts.name: Proof by Contradiction
concepts.intuition_gloss: Assume the opposite of what you want, follow the logic until the world breaks, and conclude you were right.
concepts.formal_gloss: To prove P, derive a contradiction from ¬P; since ¬P leads to absurdity, P holds.
